title: Al-Mondeel and the Definite Article 'al-' in Arabic: Sun Letters (Shamsi) vs Moon Letters (Qamariyya)
summary: The session covered vocabulary like 'al-mandeel' (handkerchief) and focused on grammar rules involving the definite article 'al-'. Key discussions included when the laam in 'al-' remains pronounced or becomes silent before specific letters. The teacher emphasized distinguishing Sun (Shamsi) and Moon (Qamariyya) letters to avoid pronunciation errors, linking this to proper Quran recitation.
key_points: The definite article 'ال' (al-) is used for specificity: ‘الكتاب’ (as-al-kitābu). When the following noun starts with a Sun Letter (Shamsiya), the laam of 'al-' becomes silent and a shaddah is added, e.g., ‘الدّار’ (ad-dāru instead of al-dāru).

28 Arabic letters split into Shamsi (Sun) and Qamariyya (Moon) letters. Sun Letters cause the laam in 'al-' to drop; examples include ت (tā'), ث (thāl), د (dāl). Moon Letters retain the laam, e.g., ق (qāf), ك (kāf).
Proper pronunciation of words like ‘الدّار’ and ‘الكتاب’ is crucial for accurate Quran recitation. Sun letters include ت, ث, د while others are Moon.
Examples from the Quran were used to highlight practical applications: ‘الرّجُل’ (al-rajulu) becomes aristocratic, while ‘الكتاب’ retains its laam.
